content.text: Tool-awareness prompting teaches the model which external functions are available, what they do, and when to invoke them. The quality of the tool description directly controls whether the model calls the right tool, passes the right arguments, and knows when not to call any tool at all. A well-described tool is one the model can use correctly on the first call; a poorly described tool leads to wrong arguments, unnecessary calls, or the model hallucinating a tool that does not exist.
Anatomy of a Good Tool Description
Field
Purpose
Common mistake
name
Unique identifier the model uses when invoking the tool
Ambiguous names (get_data, run) that do not signal intent
description
When to use this tool; what it returns; what it does NOT do
Only describing what it does, not when to use it or its limits
parameters
JSON Schema defining each argument: type, description, constraints, required vs optional
Missing descriptions on parameters; no enum for constrained values
return description
What the tool's response looks like; what fields to read
Omitting return shape; model guesses how to interpret the response
Defining Tools with the Anthropic SDK
Description Quality: Weak vs Strong
Weak description
Problem
Strong description
"Gets data from the database."
What data? When to use it?
"Returns a user's order history by user ID. Use when the user asks about past purchases. Does not return payment details."
"Search tool for finding things."
Model cannot distinguish it from other search tools
"Searches the product catalogue only. Use when the user asks about availability, specs, or pricing of products. Does not search support docs or orders."
"Sends an email."
No scope guard; model may call it too readily
"Sends a transactional email to the authenticated user only. ONLY call this after explicit user confirmation. Cannot send to third parties."

The description field does most of the work. It should answer three questions: what does the tool return, when should the model call it (positive trigger), and when should the model NOT call it (negative trigger or scope limit). Positive triggers prevent missed calls; negative triggers prevent spurious calls. Both are necessary for reliable tool use — a tool described only by what it does, without when and when-not, leaves the model to guess the boundaries.
